focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

South St. Louis came on in low brick and sodium light as Eli crossed the river. The patched radiator had started its thin kettle whine again, a high note under the engine that he could feel through the steering wheel. The sedan smelled like old coffee and the sweet chemical edge of coolant. He drove with both windows cracked because he did not trust the gauge and because the air inside the car had turned stale with his own shirt and fear.

Theo's block sat under a row of flat-roof brick buildings with narrow porches and dented mailboxes bolted crooked at shoulder height. Somebody had parked a rusting grill half on the sidewalk, half in the dirt strip where grass used to be. A television thumped through an open downstairs window in quick bursts of crowd noise and ad jingles. Eli rolled past once, then circled again because he had missed the number and because he had still not decided what words he was going to use.

He parked in front of a fire hydrant anyway. He told himself it was two minutes. He shut the engine off and sat with his hands on the wheel while the metal clicked and cooled. Nora's message thread was open on his burner. The last text from her had come at three in the afternoon.

`You do not get to disappear and then call this confusion. Return funds today.`

He could have answered. He had answered versions of that message in his head for two days, each version sounding calm and practical until he looked at the numbers in his green folder and watched calm collapse into arithmetic. Mortgage arrears. June's tuition bill. The transfer he had made from emergency savings before he left, fourteen thousand dollars moved in twelve taps while Nora was on an overnight ICU shift and June was packing dish soap and twin XL sheets.

He locked the car, took th...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...sheet when he pulled it up, old and sweet.

He opened the text thread again and thumbed in each word on the rubber keypad: `I left after move-in. I took $14,000 from our emergency account before I left. I'm in Indiana with a dead radiator.`

That was closer to true and still not enough.

If he sent it, she could call. If she called, he would hear the clock in their kitchen behind her, the one that clicked too loud at night, and he would open with the radiator crack, the tow receipt, the cash shortage, the temporary labor. By the time he got to the fourteen thousand, the call would already be bleeding out.

He set the phone on his chest and listened to trucks shift gears on the interstate. Sleep did not come. Around two he drafted another text to June and erased it before the first line finished. Around three he got up, sat at the tiny laminate desk under the mirror, and wrote figures on the back of a diner receipt.

Tow shortfall: paid. Repair total: four forty-five. Deposit on file with Darlene from Cora: one thirty-seven. Remaining repair balance: three oh eight if Darlene kept her estimate. Room: thirty-five. Fuel to St. Louis plus food: maybe one forty if he drove gentle and skipped meals that were not free. Cash on hand after room: two dollars plus loose change.

The numbers were clean. His life was not.

At four-ten he opened Nora's thread one last time, added `I'll call at six and answer whatever you ask.`, and hit SEND before he could edit himself clean.

Outside, dawn had started to thin the black above the tow-yard fence. A forklift coughed awake. Darlene's laugh carried up from below, sharp and brief. Eli lay back in clothes that smelled like bleach and fry oil, eyes open, the sent message hot in his pocket, live and mean.

He did not sleep before his alarm.
